meta.title: Ancient Greek and Roman Gods
meta.author: Cheryl Reifsnyder
meta.focusQuestion: What did the ancient Greeks believe about their gods?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: The ancient Greeks told stories about great adventures, wars, and romance. They built temples to their gods and crafted great works of art.

The ancient Greeks believed that their gods were much like humans, except larger, stronger, more beautiful, and most important—<s>immortal</s>! They were called Olympians because they lived on Mount Olympus. A gate of clouds circled its peak, allowing only the gods to pass.

Like mere mortals, the Olympians enjoyed games, music, art, and fancy jewelry; they fell in love, married, and had children. They even argued and fought with one another. Sometimes the gods <v>disguised</v> themselves as people or other animals when visiting Earth because in their true forms they glowed with such a bright golden light that humans couldn't stand the sight. The gods helped heroes, honored people who honored them, and punished those who didn't.

sections.title: Zeus, God of Sky and Weather
paragraphs: Zeus (ZOOS) was king of the Olympians, god of sky and weather, and the wisest and mightiest of the gods. All the other gods had to obey him.

Zeus had not always ruled the heavens. He led his brothers, Poseidon (poh-SY-dun) and Hades (HAY-deez), to overthrow their father, Kronos, and the other Titans. After the Titans' defeat, Zeus and his brothers divided up the world's rule. Zeus won the contest to become god of the sky. Thunder and lightning became his weapons.
The Greeks believed that Zeus protected new the kings and rulers of Earth. He watched over everyone to uphold law and protect those who were wronged.

sections.title: Poseidon, God of the Oceans
paragraphs: Poseidon was the ruler of Earth's oceans. His kingdom suited him because his mood changed as quickly as the sea. When he was angry, Poseidon summoned storms and earthquakes with his magic trident; with the same trident, he could calm the waves.

Poseidon roamed his kingdom in a golden chariot pulled by horses in the shape of waves. One story says Poseidon created the first horse while trying to impress the goddess of the harvest, Demeter. She asked him to make her a new animal, one more beautiful than any other. When he made a horse, she was delighted. Poseidon was delighted, too—so much so that he forgot Demeter in his hurry to make more horses!

sections.title: Hades, God of the Underworld
paragraphs: Hades won the realm known as the underworld. There he ruled as the god of death. He made sure the dead were properly buried. The earth's hidden wealth belonged to him as well: gold, silver, and other treasures buried underground.
Most Greek gods lived in palaces, but Hades seldom left the dark underworld. He asked Zeus to let him marry the lovely Persephone. When Zeus agreed, Hades carried her to his palace.

Persephone's mother threatened Zeus until he ordered Hades to return the girl to daylight. Hades obeyed—but only after offering Persephone food from his underworld kingdom. She ate several pomegranate seeds, which required her to return to Hades' realm for a few months each year.

sections.title: Hermes, God of Merchants
paragraphs: Hermes (HUR-meez), god of merchants, was a son of Zeus and the only god besides Hades allowed into the underworld. When someone died, Hermes guided that person to Hades' realm.

Any similarity to Hades ended there. Hades seldom spoke or smiled, but Hermes loved to talk and play tricks on people. While still a baby, he tiptoed from his mother's side to steal his brother's cows. He covered the cows' feet with bark to hide their tracks and tied brooms to their tails to sweep the ground behind them.
Hermes' quick wit and clever tongue so pleased his father that he made Hermes his messenger. Zeus gave Hermes winged sandals and a winged golden cap to speed him on his way.

sections.title: Hera, Queen of the Gods
paragraphs: Hera (HAIR-uh), the goddess of marriage and childbirth, was Zeus' wife and queen of the gods. She was one of the few allowed to argue with Zeus! Hera wore a royal crown, carried a scepter tipped with a lotus blossom, and drove a chariot pulled by peacocks.
Greek women asked her blessing at weddings. Hera was also the goddess of childbirth, thought to protect a mother when giving birth.

sections.title: Artemis, Goddess of Hunting
paragraphs: Artemis (AR-teh-mis) was the goddess of hunting, the wilderness, and wild animals. While still a child, she begged her father, Zeus, to let her stay unmarried forever. She would spend her days hunting in the mountains and forests, with the nymphs to tend her hounds.

Artemis was also a goddess of childbirth because she helped her mother give birth to her twin brother. Artemis' role as a childbirth goddess differed from Hera's: Hera protected the mother, and Artemis guarded the newborn baby.

sections.title: Hephaestus, God of Fire and Blacksmiths
paragraphs: Hephaestus (heh-FES-tus) was the god of fire, volcanoes, and blacksmiths. He was born with crippled legs, and when his mother, Hera, saw him, she was so upset that she threw him from Olympus.

Hephaestus fell to the ocean far below, where sea goddesses found him and gave him shelter. He lived with them for nine years, working as a blacksmith until he became the Olympians' most skilled craftsman. Both gods and heroes delighted in his finely crafted weapons, armor, and jewelry.

One day, Hephaestus made a marvelous throne of gold and sent it to Olympus. When Hera sat on the throne, hidden springs wedged her in place! Hephaestus had made a trap to punish her for throwing him away.

sections.title: Aphrodite, Goddess of Love and Beauty
paragraphs: Golden-haired Aphrodite (af-roh-DY-tee) was the goddess of love and beauty. She had the power to give beauty and charm to those who pleased her and make anyone fall in love. The mischievous child-god Eros—called Cupid by the Romans—was her son.

When Hera was caught by Hephaestus' trap, Zeus offered Aphrodite's hand in marriage to whoever freed his wife. Unable to resist Aphrodite's loveliness, Hephaestus came to Mount Olympus to free his mother and marry Aphrodite.
When they married, Hephaestus made Aphrodite a metal belt woven with precious gems and magic. Its power made Aphrodite completely irresistible to both gods and men.

sections.title: Ares, God of War
paragraphs: Ares (AIR-eez), the god of war, was a son of Zeus and Hera. He was a fierce, strong warrior who wielded a gigantic spear. Ares loved the destruction and smells of war. Sometimes other gods and goddesses chose one side to support in battle, but not Ares. He didn't care who was right or wrong; sometimes he even switched sides mid-battle! Ares loved fighting for its own sake.

Ares did not get along well with many of the other Olympians, most of whom hated his attitude and violence. He was often found with his sister Eris, the goddess of discord, who had the power to stir up war. He was also in love with Aphrodite, who loved the handsome god of war more than she loved her husband, Hephaestus.

sections.title: Athena, Goddess of Wisdom
paragraphs: Of all Zeus' children, the goddess of wisdom had the strangest birth. Athena sprang from Zeus' head, fully grown and dressed in golden armor! Athena became Zeus' favorite child, the only one allowed to use all his weapons—even his thunderbolts and lightning.

The Greeks believed that Athena was responsible for many types of wisdom. They gave her credit for inventing almost every type of art and craft done by women, such as spinning, weaving, pottery, and cooking. For farmers, she invented the plow and the horse's bridle. She was also considered a goddess of war and was thought to give leaders good advice.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: The Greek gods held a central role in ancient Greek society, inspiring countless sculptures, temples, and other works of art—but their influence didn't end in ancient Greece. Not far away in Italy, the Romans modeled much of their society on the Greeks'.

Even modern people look to ancient Greece for ideas from their temples and statues. The first Olympic Games, in 776 bc, were running contests held to honor the gods. They gave rise to today's Olympic Games. In fact, ancient Greece is sometimes called the birthplace of Western society. The ideas illustrated in Greek mythology helped shape the way people think and interact today.

vocabulary.definition: a horse-drawn carriage with two wheels that was used in ancient times, usually for racing and fighting battles
vocabulary.sentence: Poseidon roamed his kingdom in a golden chariot pulled by horses in the shape of waves.

options: a type of weapon used by soldiers in ancient battles and wars
a horse-drawn carriage used in ancient times for racing and battles
a sacred temple where ancient Greeks went to worship their gods
a golden crown worn by kings and queens during important ceremonies

vocabulary.definition: changed to appear to be something different so as not to be recognized
vocabulary.sentence: Sometimes the gods disguised themselves as people or other animals when visiting Earth.

options: destroyed completely by a powerful force and reduced to rubble
changed their appearance so they would not be recognized by others
made very large in size so that everyone could see them clearly
turned into solid gold by a magical power or supernatural force

vocabulary.definition: not subject to death; living forever
vocabulary.sentence: The ancient Greeks believed that their gods were much like humans, except larger, stronger, more beautiful, and most important—immortal!

options: very powerful and able to defeat any enemy in a battle
living forever and never subject to death at any point in time
extremely beautiful in a way that amazed everyone who saw them
invisible to humans and able to move around without being seen

vocabulary.definition: liking to cause trouble in a playful way
vocabulary.sentence: The mischievous child-god Eros—called Cupid by the Romans—was her son.

options: very serious and careful about following every rule and law
liking to cause trouble in a playful and lighthearted way for fun
extremely powerful and able to control forces of nature at will
beautiful and charming in a way that attracted everyone's attention

vocabulary.definition: the myths, or ancient stories about heroes or supernatural beings, of a culture or group
vocabulary.sentence: The ideas illustrated in Greek mythology helped shape the way people think and interact today.

options: ancient temples where people gathered to worship the Greek gods
ancient stories about heroes and supernatural beings of a culture
royal crowns worn by kings and queens during important ceremonies
battle weapons forged by the god Hephaestus in his fiery forge

vocabulary.definition: in stories, spirits in the shape of young women who live in mountains, forests, meadows, or water
vocabulary.sentence: She would spend her days hunting in the mountains and forests, with the nymphs to tend her hounds.

options: powerful gods who ruled over the sky, sea, and underworld
spirits in the shape of young women who live in nature
golden weapons forged by the craftsman god Hephaestus
ancient temples built on the highest peaks of mountains

vocabulary.definition: an area over which a person, such as a monarch, rules or has power
vocabulary.sentence: Hades won the realm known as the underworld.

options: a type of weapon used by warriors in battles against their enemies
an area over which a person such as a monarch rules or has power
a golden chariot pulled by horses shaped from ocean waves
a royal crown worn on the head as a symbol of authority

vocabulary.definition: a decorated rod held by a ruler as a symbol of power
vocabulary.sentence: Hera wore a royal crown, carried a scepter tipped with a lotus blossom, and drove a chariot pulled by peacocks.

options: a decorated rod held by a ruler as a symbol of royal power
a type of horse-drawn carriage used for transportation in ancient times
a magical belt woven with precious gems that gives special powers
a golden throne where the king or queen sits during ceremonies

vocabulary.definition: buildings that are places of worship
vocabulary.sentence: They built temples to their gods and crafted great works of art.

options: weapons used by soldiers in battles against enemy city-states
buildings that serve as places of worship for the ancient gods
royal chariots pulled by horses during religious ceremonies
high mountain peaks where the gods were believed to reside

vocabulary.definition: in Greek myths, large and powerful beings who ruled before the gods
vocabulary.sentence: He led his brothers, Poseidon and Hades, to overthrow their father, Kronos, and the other Titans.

options: the first Olympic athletes who competed in ancient sporting contests
large and powerful beings who ruled the world before the gods
golden weapons forged by Hephaestus in his volcanic mountain forge
sea goddesses who lived in the ocean and helped sailors navigate

vocabulary.definition: a weapon that looks like a spear with three points
vocabulary.sentence: When he was angry, Poseidon summoned storms and earthquakes with his magic trident.

options: a golden chariot pulled by horses that ran across the ocean waves
a weapon that looks like a spear with three sharp points on the end
a royal crown worn by the king or queen as a symbol of power
a magical belt woven with precious gems that makes the wearer irresistible

vocabulary.definition: the place where some of the dead go in Greek myths, ruled by the god Hades
vocabulary.sentence: Hades won the realm known as the underworld.

options: the bright sky where Zeus lived and ruled over all the other gods
the place where some of the dead go in Greek myths, ruled by Hades
a golden temple built on top of Mount Olympus for the gods to live in
the deep ocean ruled by Poseidon where storms and earthquakes began

questions.question: What is the most likely reason the author wrote Ancient Greek and Roman Gods?
questions.evidence: The ancient Greeks believed that their gods were much like humans, except larger, stronger, more beautiful, and most important—immortal!
choices: to inform readers about the ancient civilizations of Greece and Rome
to inform and entertain readers with fascinating details about Greek and Roman gods
to persuade readers to travel and visit the ancient sites in Greece and Rome
to entertain and persuade readers with humorous jokes about ancient Greek gods
questions.explanation: The author both informs (facts about gods) and entertains (exciting myths and stories).

questions.question: Which of the following words means liking to cause trouble in a playful way?
questions.evidence: Hermes was known for being clever and mischievous.
choices: mischievous
nymphs
disguised
immortal
questions.explanation: 'Mischievous' means liking to cause trouble in a playful way — like Hermes!

questions.question: What effect did the metal belt that Hephaestus made for Aphrodite have on her?
questions.evidence: Aphrodite wore the golden belt, and anyone who looked at her fell in love with her.
choices: It made her feel very sick and weak all the time.
It made her act mischievous and play tricks on others.
It made her feel happy and excited every single day.
It made her completely irresistible to everyone who saw her.
questions.explanation: The magic golden belt made Aphrodite irresistible — anyone who looked at her fell in love.

questions.question: What happened after Zeus and his brothers overthrew their father and the other Titans?
questions.evidence: After the Titans' defeat, Zeus and his brothers divided up the world's rule with a contest.
choices: They tried to destroy each other in a terrible battle for power.
Zeus lost the contest to determine who would rule the entire world.
Poseidon took his father's place as the ruler of all the Titans.
They divided up the world's rule among themselves through a contest.
questions.explanation: After overthrowing the Titans, Zeus and his brothers (Poseidon and Hades) divided up the world's rule through a contest.

questions.question: What did Hermes have in common with Hades?
questions.evidence: Any similarity to Hades ended there. Hades seldom spoke or smiled, but Hermes loved to talk and play tricks on people. Both were allowed into the underworld.
choices: They both loved to talk to people and tell funny stories.
They were both allowed into the underworld, despite their different personalities.
They both hated to talk and preferred to stay quiet and alone.
They both played tricks on people and caused trouble for fun.
questions.explanation: Both Hermes and Hades were allowed into the underworld, though they had very different personalities.

questions.question: What is the main idea of this book?
questions.evidence: The Greek gods held a central role in ancient Greek society, inspiring countless sculptures, temples, and other works of art.
choices: The ancient gods were very fun and exciting for people to believe in.
Zeus was the mightiest god of all, and all other gods had to obey him.
The ancient gods were a central part of Greek and Roman society and culture.
The ancient Greeks told many stories about the gods and their many adventures.
questions.explanation: The main idea is that the ancient gods were central to Greek and Roman society, influencing art, beliefs, and daily life.

questions.question: What caused Hera to throw Hephaestus from Olympus when he was born?
questions.evidence: When Hephaestus was born, he had crippled legs. Hera was ashamed and threw him from Olympus.
choices: He was on fire and burning everything around him.
He bit her hand when she tried to hold him.
He had crippled legs, which made Hera feel ashamed.
Springs popped out of him and hit Hera in the face.
questions.explanation: Hera threw Hephaestus from Olympus because he was born with crippled legs and she was ashamed.

questions.question: What does the word 'temples' mean as it is used in the book?
questions.evidence: They built temples to their gods and crafted great works of art.
choices: weapons that look like long spears with sharp points on the end
the flattened area on the sides of your forehead above your ears
buildings that are places of worship where people honor the gods
a decorated rod held by a ruler as a symbol of royal power
questions.explanation: In this context, 'temples' means buildings that are places of worship for the gods.

questions.question: Which of the following details has an entertaining element?
questions.evidence: Athena sprang from Zeus' head, fully grown and dressed in golden armor!
choices: Ares, the god of war, was a son of Zeus and the goddess Hera.
Athena sprang from Zeus' head, fully grown and dressed in golden armor!
Artemis was the goddess of hunting, the wilderness, and wild animals.
Poseidon was the ruler of Earth's oceans and controlled all the seas.
questions.explanation: The image of Athena springing fully grown from Zeus' head in golden armor is an entertaining, exciting detail.

questions.question: Why did Artemis beg Zeus to let her stay unmarried forever?
questions.evidence: Artemis begged Zeus to let her stay unmarried forever so she could roam the wilderness with her animals.
choices: She preferred to spend her time roaming freely in the wilderness with animals.
The other gods were mean to her and she wanted to get away from them.
She wanted to make Zeus angry by refusing to do what he asked of her.
She hated animals and the outdoors and wanted to stay inside the palace.
questions.explanation: Artemis loved the wilderness and wanted to stay unmarried so she could roam freely with her animals.

questions.question: What effect did the gods have on the Greeks and Romans? Why?
questions.evidence: The Greek gods held a central role in ancient Greek society, inspiring countless sculptures, temples, and other works of art. The ideas illustrated in Greek mythology helped shape the way people think and interact today.
questions.explanation: The gods were central to Greek and Roman society — they inspired art, temples, and shaped how people think and act today.
opinionPrompts: What evidence shows the gods were important?
How do we still see their influence today?

questions.question: Do you agree that being immortal was the most important characteristic of the gods? Why or why not? Use details from the book to support your answer.
questions.evidence: The ancient Greeks believed that their gods were much like humans, except larger, stronger, more beautiful, and most important—immortal!
questions.explanation: Think about whether living forever was the most important quality, or if other traits (strength, beauty, powers) were more significant.
opinionPrompts: Why might immortality be most important?

authorPurpose.prompt: What was the author's purpose for writing this book? Check all that apply and find evidence from the text.

evidencePrompts.Inform: Write details from the text that show the author is giving information:
evidencePrompts.Entertain: Write details from the text that show the author is trying to entertain:
evidencePrompts.Persuade: Write details from the text that show the author is trying to persuade:

answerKey.Inform: The book gives factual information about each god — their domains, symbols, Roman names, and stories. Example: 'Zeus was king of the Olympians, god of sky and weather.'
answerKey.Entertain: The book tells exciting stories and myths about the gods. Example: Athena springing from Zeus' head fully grown and dressed in golden armor, or Hermes stealing cows as a baby.
answerKey.Persuade: The author does NOT seem to be trying to persuade readers of anything — this is primarily an informational and entertaining text.

prompts.K: What do you already know about ancient Greek and Roman gods?
prompts.W: What do you want to learn about ancient Greek and Roman gods?
prompts.L: What did you learn from reading this book?
prompts.S: What do you still want to know about ancient Greek and Roman gods?

L: The gods were immortal — they lived forever.
Zeus was the king of all gods and controlled the sky.
Each god had a specific domain (area of power).
The Romans adopted the Greek gods with different names.
Greek mythology still influences modern society today.

pronounAgreement.original: Zeus led his brothers, Poseidon and Hades, to overthrow their father and the other Titans.
pronounAgreement.hint: Replace 'his brothers, Poseidon and Hades' with a pronoun.

pronounAgreement.original: Zeus upheld law and protected those who were wronged.
pronounAgreement.hint: Replace 'those who were wronged' with a pronoun.

pronounAgreement.original: When Poseidon made a horse, Demeter was delighted.
The earth's hidden wealth belonged to Hades as well.
pronounAgreement.hint: Replace 'The earth's hidden wealth' with a pronoun.

pronounAgreement.original: Artemis would spend her days hunting in the mountains and forests.
When Hera sat on Hera's throne, hidden springs wedged her in place!
The Greeks believed that Athena was responsible for many types of wisdom.

intro.title: Immortal
intro.content: Living forever, never dying. The gods were immortal but humans were not!

intro.title: Disguised
intro.content: Changed appearance to hide who you really are. The gods disguised themselves as animals or people!

intro.title: Author's Purpose
intro.content: Why did the author write this? To INFORM (give facts), ENTERTAIN (tell fun stories), or PERSUADE (convince you)? This book does the first two!

zeus.title: Titans
zeus.content: In Greek myths, the Titans were large, powerful beings who ruled BEFORE the gods. Zeus overthrew them!

zeus.title: Pronoun Agreement
zeus.content: 'He led HIS brothers' — 'His' is a pronoun that refers to Zeus. The pronoun must agree with what it replaces!

poseidon.title: Trident
poseidon.content: A three-pointed spear weapon. Poseidon's trident could create storms OR calm the seas!

poseidon.title: Chariot
poseidon.content: A two-wheeled horse-drawn carriage used in ancient times for racing and battles.

hades.title: Underworld
hades.content: The place where the dead go in Greek myths. Hades ruled this dark realm underground.

hades.title: Realm
hades.content: An area that a ruler controls. Hades' realm was the underworld.

hermes.title: Compare & Contrast
hermes.content: Hermes and Hades are both allowed in the underworld, BUT they're very different! Hermes is talkative, Hades is quiet. This is comparing and contrasting!

hera.title: Scepter
hera.content: A decorated rod held by a ruler as a symbol of power. Like a king's special stick!

artemis.title: Nymphs
artemis.content: Nature spirits in the shape of young women. They lived in mountains, forests, and water.

hephaestus.title: Pronoun Agreement
hephaestus.content: 'Hephaestus fell...where sea goddesses found HIM' — 'Him' refers back to Hephaestus. This is called the antecedent!

aphrodite.title: Mischievous
aphrodite.content: Liking to cause trouble in a playful way. Eros (Cupid) was mischievous!

conclusion.title: Mythology
conclusion.content: Ancient stories about heroes and supernatural beings. Greek mythology still influences us today!
